nativeRange.habitat: Peat swamp forest streams and slow-moving blackwater channels with heavily tannin-stained water, leaf litter substrate, minimal light penetration, and negligible mineral content; Jambi and Riau provinces of Sumatra (Indonesia) and Johor state of Peninsular Malaysia

decorPreferences: driftwood and twisted roots for cover
dense floating plants (frogbit, salvinia) to dim lighting
clay pots or hollow caves as spawning sites
dim lighting — avoid bright overhead illumination
tight-fitting lid (labyrinth organ fry require humid air layer)

diet.notes: Primarily small live or frozen invertebrates: Daphnia, Artemia (brine shrimp), bloodworms, and micro-worms. Wild fish feed on zooplankton, insect larvae, and small aquatic invertebrates. Will accept high-quality micro-pellets and freeze-dried foods once acclimated, but live and frozen foods are strongly preferred for long-term health and conditioning for breeding. Avoid overfeeding — small, frequent meals suit this species better than single large feedings.

goodWith: own species (single pair or small group in a well-planted, spacious tank)
small peaceful blackwater tetras (ember tetras, chili rasboras)
small corydoras in appropriately soft water
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving fish that will outcompete for food

summary: The type species of the Coccina group, this jewel-like wild Betta from Sumatran blackwater peat swamps displays deep wine-red to crimson coloration. A bubblenester requiring very soft, acid water; best kept as a dedicated species pair. Intermediate care due to strict water chemistry demands.
careNotes:
Betta coccina is the type species of the Coccina complex, a group of small wild Bettas from blackwater peat swamp environments in Sumatra and Peninsular Malaysia. Success with this species hinges almost entirely on water chemistry: extremely soft (gH 0–4, kH 0–2), acidic water (pH 4.0–6.0) is not merely preferred but essential. Hard or alkaline tap water without extensive preparation will cause chronic stress and early death. Reverse osmosis or deionised water re-mineralised lightly with peat extract, Indian almond leaf tannins, or dedicated blackwater conditioner is the standard approach.

A minimum tank of 40 L with a 45 cm footprint suits a single pair; a larger, well-planted 60–80 L allows a trio or small group. Filtration must be gentle — a small sponge filter providing 4–5× turnover is ideal. Bright lighting stresses this fish; floating plant coverage and leaf litter are essential to replicate the near-lightless peat swamp floor. A tight-fitting lid is mandatory: Betta coccina is an accomplished jumper, and fry require a humid air layer above the water surface for proper labyrinth organ development.

Conspecific aggression between males can occur, particularly in small tanks. A single pair is the safest approach for smaller setups; groups can work in a heavily planted, larger aquarium with multiple visual breaks. Mixed-species tanks are possible but must use similarly sized, peaceful blackwater fish. This species is listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List (2018) due to deforestation and peat swamp drainage in its native range; captive-bred specimens are strongly preferred over wild-caught.
breedingNotes: Betta coccina is a bubblenester — males construct a floating bubble nest, often anchored beneath a broad leaf or at the water surface under floating plants. Conditioning pairs on live or frozen foods (Daphnia, Artemia, bloodworms) for 1–2 weeks before introducing them triggers spawning. Typical clutches are small, with fewer than 20 eggs common, though broods up to 50 have been reported. Eggs are deposited in the nest by the female and tended exclusively by the male; the female should be removed after spawning to prevent male aggression. Fry hatch in 24–48 hours and become free-swimming a few days later. The humid air layer above the water surface is critical in the first weeks of life for proper labyrinth organ development — do not lower water level excessively or leave gaps that permit cold drafts. First foods: infusoria, micro-worms, and newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii. Breeding water must replicate blackwater conditions (pH 4.5–5.5, near-zero hardness, temperature 26–28°C).

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Betta coccina
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (35–40 mm SL), temperature (22–30°C), pH (4.0–6.0), hardness (18–90 ppm ≈ gH 1–5), minimum tank footprint (45×30 cm), native range (Jambi and Riau provinces, Sumatra; Johor, Peninsular Malaysia), habitat (peat swamp forest blackwater), diet (small live/frozen invertebrates), breeding details (bubblenester, <20 eggs typical, male guards, hatches 24–48 h), and husbandry notes (tight lid, humid air layer, dim light, sponge filter).

additional.name: FishBase — Betta coccina
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 5.6 cm TL), temperature (24–27°C), pH (4.0–6.0), hardness (dH 0–4), native range (Malaysia and Indonesia), taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes, subfamily Macropodusinae), IUCN status (Vulnerable, 2018), and facultative air-breathing via labyrinth organ. FishBase max 5.6 cm TL used as size ceiling; SeriouslyFish 35–40 mm SL used as typical range.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Betta coccina
additional.notes: Corroborated common names (Wine Red Betta, Red Fighting Fish), adult size (5–5.6 cm), peat swamp soft-acid habitat, bubblenesting breeding behaviour, and IUCN Vulnerable status. Noted species described by Jörg Vierke, 1979. Confirmed diet: small invertebrates and zooplankton in the wild.

fish.breedingNotes: Bubblenester; male constructs nest beneath floating plants or broad leaves. Typical clutch <20 eggs, up to 50. Female removed post-spawning; male guards until free-swimming. Fry require humid air layer for labyrinth organ development. Condition on live/frozen foods; blackwater chemistry (pH 4.5–5.5, near-zero hardness) essential for successful spawning.
